help.title: Help & Support
help.subtitle: Find answers to common questions about each tool and how to get the most out of ORDEX Systems.
help.needHelpDesc: If you can't find the answer you're looking for, reach out to our support team. We typically respond within 24 hours on business days.

general.a1: Credits are consumed when you run computational jobs (predictions, docking, AI analyses). Each tool shows the estimated credit cost before you submit a job. Credits are shared across your organization and can be purchased from the Billing & Usage page.
general.a2: Your organization's credit balance is too low for the requested job. Purchase additional credits from Billing & Usage, or ask your organization admin to do so. You can also check your current balance in the top bar.
general.q3: How do I invite team members?
general.a3: Go to the Team page and use the invite form to send invitations by email. Invited users will receive a link to join your organization. Only admins can invite new members.
general.q4: Can I change my organization/workspace name?
general.a4: Yes — go to Settings → Workspace and update the workspace name. Only organization admins can make this change.
general.a5: Sessions automatically refresh in the background. If you're frequently logged out, check that your browser isn't blocking cookies or clearing storage. Try disabling browser extensions that manage cookies.
general.q6: How is my data stored and secured?
general.a6: All data is stored in encrypted databases with row-level security. Each user can only access their own data and data shared within their organization. We do not share your data with third parties.

protein.title: Protein Prediction
protein.desc: Predict 3D protein structures from amino acid sequences using AlphaFold-based models.
protein.q1: My prediction job is stuck in 'Queued' status
protein.a1: Jobs are processed in order. During peak usage, queue times may increase. If a job remains queued for more than 30 minutes, try cancelling and resubmitting. Ensure your sequence is valid — only standard amino acid letters (A-Z) are accepted.
protein.a2: Enter a raw amino acid sequence using single-letter codes (e.g. MKTLLILAVL...). FASTA headers are automatically stripped. Sequences must be between 10 and 2,700 residues.
protein.q3: The pLDDT score is very low
protein.a3: A low pLDDT score (< 50) indicates the model has low confidence in the predicted structure. This often happens with intrinsically disordered regions, very short peptides, or sequences with no known homologs. Try a longer or more complete sequence.
protein.a4: Currently, single-chain predictions are supported. For multi-chain or protein-protein interactions, consider using the Molecular Docking tool with individual predicted structures.
protein.q5: My job failed with an error
protein.a5: Common causes: invalid characters in the sequence, sequence too long (>2,700 residues), or insufficient credits. Check the error message in the job details for specifics. You can retry failed jobs without being charged again.

docking.title: Molecular Docking
docking.desc: Simulate how small molecules (ligands) bind to protein targets using AutoDock Vina.
docking.q1: What file formats are supported for receptor/ligand upload?
docking.a1: Receptors: PDB or PDBQT format. Ligands: SDF, MOL2, or SMILES strings. You can also search for ligands by name using the built-in PubChem integration.
docking.a2: Docking scores are approximate binding energies (kcal/mol). More negative = stronger predicted binding. Scores above -4.0 generally indicate weak binding. Ensure your receptor structure is clean — remove water molecules and co-crystallized ligands before uploading.
docking.q3: How do I set the binding site?
docking.a3: Use 'Auto-detect pocket' to let the algorithm find the most likely binding site, or specify coordinates manually if you know the active site location. Auto-detection works best with well-resolved crystal structures.
docking.q4: Batch docking jobs are timing out
docking.a4: Large batch jobs (>50 ligands) may take significant time. Break very large batches into smaller groups of 20-30 ligands. Each ligand in a batch consumes credits independently.
docking.a5: Yes — click on a completed job to view the top-scoring poses in the built-in 3D viewer. You can also export poses and load them in the dedicated 3D Viewer tool for more detailed analysis.

synbio.title: SynBio Design
synbio.desc: Design and validate synthetic DNA/RNA constructs with codon optimization and annotation.
synbio.a1: Codon optimization adjusts the DNA sequence encoding your protein to use codons preferred by your target host organism (e.g. E. coli, yeast, CHO cells), improving expression levels without changing the protein sequence.
synbio.q2: My GC content is flagged as problematic
synbio.a2: Ideal GC content is typically 40-60%. Extreme GC content can cause issues with DNA synthesis, PCR amplification, and expression. The tool highlights regions outside this range so you can adjust your design.
synbio.a3: Gibson Assembly, Golden Gate, restriction cloning, and overlap extension PCR. The tool generates appropriate overhangs and validates compatibility for your chosen method.
synbio.q4: The CAI score is low after optimization
synbio.a4: A Codon Adaptation Index (CAI) below 0.7 suggests suboptimal codon usage. Try selecting a different optimization organism or adjusting the sequence manually. Some sequences with rare amino acid compositions may not achieve high CAI scores.
synbio.q5: Can I annotate features on my construct?
synbio.a5: Yes — the design tool automatically detects common features (promoters, RBS, terminators, resistance markers). You can also add custom annotations to mark specific regions of interest.

crispr.title: CRISPR Lab
crispr.desc: Design guide RNAs, analyze off-target effects, and manage gene editing experiments.
crispr.a1: Off-target analysis searches for genomic locations with high similarity to your guide sequence plus PAM. Each potential off-target is scored based on the number and position of mismatches. Lower off-target scores indicate higher specificity.
crispr.a2: SpCas9 (NGG PAM), SaCas9 (NNGRRT PAM), Cas12a/Cpf1 (TTTV PAM), and engineered variants like xCas9. Select your variant before designing guides to ensure correct PAM recognition.
crispr.q3: My guide has low efficiency but high specificity
crispr.a3: This is a common trade-off. Guides near the 5' end of genes or in open chromatin regions tend to have higher efficiency. Consider testing 2-3 guides and selecting the best performer experimentally.
crispr.a4: Yes — create separate experiments for each target gene or project. Each experiment maintains its own guide designs, edit logs, and version history. Use the log feature to record experimental observations and results.
crispr.q5: How do I export my guide designs?
crispr.a5: Guide sequences, PAM sites, and off-target results can be copied directly from the interface. Full experiment data including all guides and logs can be reviewed in the experiment detail view.

cellculture.title: CellCulture AI
cellculture.desc: AI-powered monitoring and optimization for cell culture experiments.
cellculture.q1: What data should I log for best AI predictions?
cellculture.a1: Log as many parameters as possible: confluence %, viability %, pH, glucose level, lactate level, cell count, and morphology notes. The more data points over time, the more accurate the AI's analysis and recommendations.
cellculture.a2: AI analyses are based on the data you provide and general cell culture best practices. They should be used as a starting point, not as a definitive protocol. Always validate recommendations against your specific cell line's known behavior.
cellculture.a3: Any adherent or suspension cell line can be tracked. Common presets include HEK293, CHO, HeLa, and Jurkat. You can enter custom cell line names and adjust default culture parameters (temperature, CO₂, humidity) accordingly.
cellculture.q4: How often should I log culture data?
cellculture.a4: For best results, log data at each passage and any time you observe significant changes. Daily logging of key metrics (confluence, viability) enables trend analysis and early detection of culture drift.
cellculture.q5: Each AI analysis costs credits — how much?
cellculture.a5: Standard AI analyses cost 3 credits per run. The credit cost is shown before you confirm. More complex analyses (multi-culture comparisons, trend predictions) may cost more.

viewer.title: 3D Viewer
viewer.desc: Interactive molecular visualization for PDB structures with annotation support.
viewer.q1: How do I load a structure?
viewer.a1: Enter a PDB ID (e.g. '1CRN', '6LU7') in the search field to fetch directly from the RCSB Protein Data Bank. Structures from your Protein Prediction jobs can also be loaded directly.
viewer.q2: The viewer is blank or shows a black screen
viewer.a2: The 3D viewer requires WebGL support. Ensure hardware acceleration is enabled in your browser settings. Try refreshing the page. On mobile devices, complex structures may render slowly.
viewer.q3: Can I change the visualization style?
viewer.a3: Yes — toggle between cartoon, stick, sphere, and surface representations. You can also color by chain, secondary structure, or B-factor/pLDDT score.
viewer.q4: How do I save notes on a structure?
viewer.a4: Use the notes panel on the right side of the viewer. Notes are saved per PDB ID and persist across sessions. This is useful for annotating binding sites, mutations, or regions of interest.
viewer.q5: Can I compare two structures side by side?
viewer.a5: Currently, the viewer displays one structure at a time. To compare structures, open multiple browser tabs with different PDB IDs loaded.
